poem-1-3.note: Yumen Pass (Jade Gate) — a Han Dynasty border pass northwest of Dunhuang, an obligatory choke point on the northern Silk Road. Wang Zhihuan's line 'the spring wind does not cross Yumen Pass' made it immortal.

poem-1-4.note: The Great Northwest — a general term for northwestern China, including Xinjiang, Gansu, Qinghai, Ningxia, and Shaanxi. Post-1949, vast numbers of youth and soldiers were sent here to develop the region.

poem-1-5.note: Dugout (dìwōzi) — semi-underground shelters constructed and inhabited by early military settlers in Xinjiang, offering essential protection from fierce sandstorms and extreme temperature variations.

poem-1-6.note: Tianshan Mountains — a massive mountain system in Central Asia traversing the entirety of central Xinjiang, stretching about 2,500 kilometers and acting as the region's geographical backbone.

poem-1-7.note: Red Willow (Tamarisk) — a remarkably drought-resistant desert shrub with deep roots. In Junggar poetry, it is the foremost symbol of the tenacity and vitality of the frontier settlers.

poem-1-13.note: Swan Geese of Harvest — a poetic metaphor serving as messengers bringing news of a bumper crop to distant families, adapting the ancient motif of 'swan geese carrying letters' to the modern reclamation context.

poem-1-17.note: Hanhai ('Vast Sea') — a classical poetic term referring to the boundless northern deserts or Gobi expanses, specifically the immense Gurbantünggüt Desert in this context.

poem-1-18.note: Pioneers (tuōhuāngzhě) — referring to the soldiers and youth who became farmers, reclaiming barren desert wastelands for agriculture within the Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps.

poem-2-1.note: Before Dawn — a poem honoring martyrs who died right before the founding of the new state. 'Dawn' metaphorically represents the peaceful liberation of Xinjiang in September 1949.

poem-2-3.note: Prometheus — the Titan from Greek mythology who stole fire for humanity. The poet uses this metaphor to praise the revolutionary pioneers who brought light and hope to the darkness of Xinjiang.

poem-2-9.note: Battlements — defensive walls on a fortress. Here, the 'emerald battlements' symbolically refer to the green forest belts planted by the soldiers to protect the newly built desert oasis city.

poem-3-1.note: Gurbantünggüt — China's second largest desert, located in the center of the Junggar Basin in northern Xinjiang, featuring vast expanses of fixed and semi-fixed sand dunes.

poem-3-3.note: Silk Road — the ancient network of trade routes connecting China to Central and West Asia, and Europe, named for the Chinese silk that was heavily traded westward.

poem-4-1.note: Nightingale — a songbird frequently found in Xinjiang's red willow thickets. In Li Yu's poetry, it is an enduring symbol of love, lyricism, and melancholy, central to the Junggar imagery.

poem-4-11.note: Snow Waves — referring to the visual of snowmelt streams plunging from the Tianshan Mountains into the desert margins. It serves as a striking metaphor for vitality and impassioned emotion.
